title.undefined: Sustainable & Circular Production of Mineral Critical Raw Materials and Mining Waste Facility  (SCIMIN_CRM & MWF) Ontology

author:
Rajesh PIRYANI, Researcher AKKODIS Research
Sadeq ZOUGARI, Researcher AKKODIS Research

description.undefined: It is an ontology for Sustainable and Circular Production of Critical Mineral Raw Materials and Mining Waste Facilities. It aims to represent the key concepts and relationships involved in the life cycle of Mining Waste Facilities (MWFs). By capturing this life cycle—from exploration and exploitation to closure—the ontology enables decision support and reasoning within the SCIMIN-CRM platform.

comment.undefined: Engineering analysis to determine the properties of a site's soil and rock, identifying conditions, risks, and mechanical properties to ensure the stability and safety of a construction or infrastructure project.
International and national guidelines and norms for conducting LCA in the mining sector.

comment.en:
MWEI BREF : Area under the control of an operator where mining occurs (see "mining"), including common related infrastructure and waste management activities.

INSPIRE: An excavation carried out for the extraction of mineral deposits.

comment.undefined: A set of rules, procedures, or guidelines agreed upon and recognized by a community or industry to ensure consistency, interoperability, and efficiency in various situations

comment.en:
Compaction of minerals includes the technologies for moving and densifying minerals by vibration, impaction and pressure, mainly used in construction applications.

MWEI BREF : Process resulting in a reduction in volume. The change typically results from externally applied loads, creating tighter packing of the solid particles. In fine soils in particular, this requires an egress of pore water. Greater compaction often results in increased consolidation

comment.undefined: Metrics that translate collected data into representative information that can be used in decision-making

comment.en:
Any change caused by a proposed activity to land, water, air and biodiversity. 
The environmental effects identified and quantified through LCA in relation to mining operations.

MWEI BREF : The direct or indirect introduction, as a result of human activity, of  substances, vibrations, heat or noise into air, water or land which may be harmful to human health or the quality of the environment, result in damage to material property, or impair or interfere with amenities and other legitimate uses of the environment.

comment.undefined: Specific events involving hazardous substance release or exposure, including their nature and management
The impact of a proposed activity on the extent and condition of habitats
The process of digging or removing tailings, which may release hazardous substances into the air
A substance or compound added to a system to cause a chemical reaction or test for the presence of another substance
process of determining the chemical composition of a substance using a range of methods and instruments to identify, characterize, and quantify its components
Potential harm to the environment caused by hazardous substances or activities

comment.en: MWEI BREF : Process of removing water from an underground mine or open pit, or from the  surrounding rock or non-lithified area. The term is also commonly used for the reduction of water content in concentrates, extractive waste from mineral  processing and treatment sludge. In hydrocarbon extraction, dewatering may  refer to removing water from water-based drilling muds and cuttings.

comment.undefined: It is work required to take a sample

comment.en: Grinding is the stage of size reduction (wet or dry) where the liberation size for individual minerals can be reached. By further size reduction filler (mineral powder) is produced.

A mining waste facility is a designated site or structure used to store extractive waste (includes materials like waste rock and tailings from mining and mineral processing operations).

MWEI BREF : Any area under the responsibility of an operator where extractive waste is  handled, treated, accumulated or deposited whether in a solid or liquid state or  in solution or suspension. This includes, but is not limited to, Extractive 
Waste Facilities, excavation voids where extractive waste is placed back, and  areas for temporary storage of extractive waste.

Mining Waste (INSPIRE Model)
Mining waste can be defined as a part of the materials that result from the exploration, mining and processing of substances governed by legislation on mines and quarries.

comment.undefined: Once a mineral sample has been taken, a series of tasks must be carried out to allow its subsequent analysis.
The potential financial loss or cost associated with hazardous substance management or incidents
Guideline defining how to decide on management measures that create the best possible positive outcome for habitat extent and condition
Guideline defining the scope and purpose of a desktop proximity analysis, and how to use its output
Documented or potential effects of recovery operations on the health of nearby populations

comment.en: geophysical process of mathematically modelling subsurface electrical resistivity distributions from measured geoelectrical data

MWEI BREF : Extraction of soluble components from raw materials by the action of a percolating liquid (e.g. water). For example, gold can be extracted by heap leaching of a porous ore, or pulverised extractive waste from mineral processing. Other methods are tank leaching of ore, concentrates or extractive waste from mineral processing and in-situ leaching. Leaching may also be an unwanted process leading to polluted drainage (see "leachate"). Bio-leaching is a particular form of leaching involving living organisms

comment.undefined: It is the classification of a mineral's size. A particle size analysis determines the proportion of minerals in different fractions.
What machinery/equipment is suitable to achieve desired particle size.

comment.en:
Mineral is a natural inorganic substance precisely defined  according to its physical and chemical characteristics.

INSPIRE: Mineral is defined as any naturally occurring inorganic element or compound having a periodically repeating arrangement of atoms and a characteristic chemical composition or range of compositions, resulting in distinctive physical properties.

Drilling (and blasting) is the technology of achieving primary fragmentation of “in situ” minerals. This is the starting point for  most mineral processes with the exception of natural minerals in the form of sand and gravel.

MWEI BREF : Facility where mineral processing is carried out.
Mineral processing (also beneficiation or benefication, ore dressing, mineral dressing, milling) 
Processes to produce marketable mineral products (e.g. concentrates) from mineral resources. This is usually carried out on the mine or quarry site, the plant being referred to as the mineral processing plant (mill or concentrator). The essential purpose is to reduce the bulk of the ore, which must be transported to and processed by subsequent processes (e.g. smelting), by using methods to separate the valuable (desired) mineral(s) from the gangue. 
The marketable product of this is called concentrate, the remaining material is called extractive waste from mineral processing (e.g. tailings).
Mineral processing includes various procedures that rely on the mineral's physical characteristics (i.e. particle size, density, magnetic properties, colour) or physico-chemical properties (surface tension, hydrophobicity, wettability).
Slurry processing includes the technologies for wet processing of mineral fractions.

comment.undefined: What machinery/equipment is suitable to achieve desired particle size.
Any material that poses a threat to human health or the environment due to its chemical or physical properties
Predefined procedures for responding to hazardous substances releases or other emergencies

comment.en:
Crushing and screening is the first controlled size reduction stage in the process. This is the main process in aggregate  production and a preparation process for further size reduction.

MWEI BREF : Comminution process that reduces the particle size of ore to such a level that  grinding can be carried out. Usually, this is accomplished by compression of  ore against rigid surfaces, or by impact against surfaces in a rigidly constrained motion path.

MWEI BREF : Screening Separating material into size fractions.

comment.en: An ore is a mineral or rock containing metal ore mineral concentrations that can be economically extracted. The ore is  the source from where the metal or other mineral substances are extracted

comment.undefined: It is an Activity

comment.en: Materials handling includes the technologies for moving the process flow (dry) forward by loading, transportation, storage  and feeding

comment.undefined: LCA is a method for evaluating environmental impacts across the entire life cycle of products, processes, or services.
Catalog of restoration activities and their relative success in positively impacting species and habitats under different abiotic, biotic, and landscape conditions

comment.en:
MWEI BREF : Operation during which ore is treated for the recovery and/or concentration of valuable minerals prior to shipment to a smelter or refinery. Milling
processes include crushing, grinding, screening, concentration and dewatering. At a coal mine, the mill is referred to as a wash plant, tipple or cleaner

comment.undefined: License or consent to do or say something.

comment.en: Pyro processing includes the technologies for upgrading of the mineral fractions by drying, calcining or sintering.

comment.undefined: Laboratory analyses are essential to deepen the characterization of materials present in MWFs, aiming to evaluate their potential for recovery or management.

comment.en:
MWEI BREF : Process of reducing ore into smaller fragments to prepare it for further  processing and/or so that it can be transported to the processing plant. In 
underground mines, the primary crusher is often located underground or at the 
entrance to the processing plant.

comment.undefined: The relationship between reclamation and its impact on species presence and extinction risk, habitat extent and condition
Potential harm to human health due to exposure to hazardous substances or unsafe conditions
When the deposit is in operation
The impact of a proposed activity on the presence or absence of species and their level of extinction risk
Laws and regulations that may limit or condition recovery operations
Guideline defining how to decide on management measures that create the best possible positive outcome for species presence and reducing extinction risk
Waste material remaining after recovery processes, which may still contain hazardous substances

comment.en: Rock is an aggregate of one or several minerals, forming the great  mass of the earth’s crust. In certain cases, rock may consist of  one single mineral as in the cases of limestone, which consists  only of calcite, stratified clayish rock and quartzite layers, etc.  Rocks may be solid, like granite, or unconsolidated like sand.  Normally, rocks are formed by more than one mineral. Some  of the minerals are predominant and form the essential  components, and others, in smaller proportions, constitute the  accessory minerals.

comment.undefined: INSPIRE: EarthMaterial is an abstract class which holds a description of a naturally occurring substance in the Earth. Earth Material represents material composition or substance, and is thus independent of quantity or location.
Harmful substances suspended in the air, such as heavy metals or silica, that pose inhalation risk
Standard procedures and guidelines designed to protect workers from health and safety risks

comment.en:
MWEI BREF : Waste resulting from the prospecting, extraction, treatment and storage of mineral resources and the working of quarries.

Mining activity exploits a mineral deposit, obtaining a concentrate (mineral object of the activity) and waste.

comment.undefined: Any change caused by a proposed activity to species and habitats, ecosystems and landscapes

LCA in mining is a systematic method for identifying, quantifying, and evaluating the environmental impacts associated with the entire life cycle of mining-related processes and products, from exploration and extraction to processing, transportation, use, and end-of-life management.
Techniques or technologies used to reduce exposure to hazardous substances, such as water sprays or enclosures
The probability and severity of adverse effects resulting from exposure to a hazard

comment.en:
MWEI BREF : Mineral processing (also beneficiation or benefication, ore dressing, mineral dressing, milling) 
Processes to produce marketable mineral products (e.g. concentrates) from mineral resources. This is usually carried out on the mine or quarry site, the plant being referred to as the mineral processing plant (mill or concentrator). .The essential purpose is to reduce the bulk of the ore, which must be transported to and processed by subsequent processes (e.g. smelting), by using methods to separate the valuable (desired) mineral(s) from the gangue. 
The marketable product of this is called concentrate, the remaining material is called extractive waste from mineral processing (e.g. tailings).
Mineral processing includes various procedures that rely on the mineral's physical characteristics (i.e. particle size, density, magnetic properties, colour) or physico-chemical properties (surface tension, hydrophobicity, wettability).

comment.undefined: Determines the types and relative amounts of minerals within a material, providing insights into its composition, properties, and formation conditions
non-invasive imaging technique that creates 2D or 3D images of an object's electrical properties by injecting electric current and measuring the voltage differences

LCA Mining Requirements
The technical, methodological, and data-related prerequisites for conducting an accurate and transparent LCA in the mining sector, including access to reliable data, clearly defined system boundaries, functional
The amount and density of airborne particulate matter released during excavation activities
This term is analogus to structure of deposit/mine
The particle size distribution of MWF material
A reagent that poses risks to health or the environment due to its toxic, corrosive or reactive properties
The particle size of MWF material
Both the scale that a proposed activity takes up in the landscape as well as its environmental impact outside its boundary
Relevant data collection and associated metrics that translate into representative information that can be used to define whether restoration has a positive impact on biodiversity
A safer or more sustainable substitute for a hazardous reagent used in industrial processes
Dense elements or compound, often metals, found in tailings that may pose environmental or health risks
Waste material remaining after recovery processes, which may still contain hazardous substances

LCA Mining Situations

Different contexts or application areas in which LCA is used in mining.
